Hypocrisy, like the spider's web, is fine-spun, flimsy, and woven out of its own inventions, as the spider's web out of its own bowels. An Arab proverb says, "Time destroys the well-built house, as well as the spider's web."

data.verse_text: He is a well-watered plant in the sunshine, spreading its shoots over the garden.
data.commentary_text: before the sun--that is, he (the godless) is green only before the sun rises; but he cannot bear its heat, and withers. So succulent plants like the gourd (Jon 4:7-8). But the widespreading in the garden does not quite accord with this. Better, "in sunshine"; the sun representing the smiling fortune of the hypocrite, during which he wondrously progresses [UMBREIT]. The image is that of weeds growing in rank luxuriance and spreading over even heaps of stones and walls, and then being speedily torn away.

data.verse_text: His roots wrap around the rock heap; he looks for a home among the stones.
data.commentary_text: seeth the place of stones--Hebrew, "the house of stones"; that is, the wall surrounding the garden. The parasite plant, in creeping towards and over the wall--the utmost bound of the garden--is said figuratively to "see" or regard it.

data.verse_text: If he is uprooted from his place, it will disown him, saying, ‘I never saw you.’
data.commentary_text: If He (God) tear him away (properly, "to tear away rapidly and violently") from his place, "then it [the place personified] shall deny him" (Psa 103:16). The very soil is ashamed of the weeds lying withered on its surface, as though it never had been connected with them. So, when the godless falls from prosperity, his nearest friends disown him.

data.verse_text: Surely this is the joy of his way; yet others will spring from the dust.
data.commentary_text:
Bitter irony. The hypocrite boasts of joy. This then is his "joy" at the last. 

and out of the earth--others immediately, who take the place of the man thus punished; not godly men (Mat 3:9). For the place of the weeds is among stones, where the gardener wishes no plants. But, ungodly; a fresh crop of weeds always springs up in the place of those torn up: there is no end of hypocrites on earth [UMBREIT].

data.verse_text: Behold, God does not reject the blameless, nor will He strengthen the hand of evildoers.
data.commentary_text:
Bildad regards Job as a righteous man, who has fallen into sin. 

God will not cast away a perfect man--(or godly man, such as Job was), if he will only repent. Those alone who persevere in sin God will not help (Hebrew, "take by the hand," Psa 73:23; Isa 41:13; Isa 42:6) when fallen.

data.verse_text: He will yet fill your mouth with laughter, and your lips with a shout of joy.
data.commentary_text: Till--literally, "to the point that"; God's blessing on thee, when repentant, will go on increasing to the point that, or until, &c.

data.verse_text: Your enemies will be clothed in shame, and the tent of the wicked will be no more.”
data.commentary_text:
The haters of Job are the wicked. They shall be clothed with shame (Jer 3:25; Psa 35:26; Psa 109:29), at the failure of their hope that Job would utterly perish, and because they, instead of him, come to naught.




Next: Job Chapter 9

data.verse_text: Then Job answered: “Yes, I know that it is so, but how can a mortal be righteous before God?
data.commentary_text:
REPLY OF JOB TO BILDAD. (Job 9:1-35)

I know it is so of a truth--that God does not "pervert justice" (Job 8:3). But (even though I be sure of being in the right) how can a mere man assert his right--(be just) with God. The Gospel answers (Rom 3:26).

data.verse_text: If one wished to contend with God, he could not answer Him one time out of a thousand.
data.commentary_text:
If he--God 

will contend with him--literally, "deign to enter into judgment." 

he cannot answer, &c.--He (man) would not dare, even if he had a thousand answers in readiness to one question of God's, to utter one of them, from awe of His Majesty.

data.verse_text: God is wise in heart and mighty in strength. Who has resisted Him and prospered?
data.commentary_text:
wise in heart--in understanding!--and mighty in power! God confounds the ablest arguer by His wisdom, and the mightiest by His power. 

hardened himself--or his neck (Pro 29:1); that is, defied God. To prosper, one must fall in with God's arrangements of providence and grace.

data.verse_text: He moves mountains without their knowledge and overturns them in His anger.
data.commentary_text: and they know not--Hebrew for "suddenly, unexpectedly, before they are aware of it" (Psa 35:8); "at unawares"; Hebrew, which "he knoweth not of" (Joe 2:14; Pro 5:6).

data.verse_text: He shakes the earth from its place, so that its foundations tremble.
data.commentary_text: The earth is regarded, poetically, as resting on pillars, which tremble in an earthquake (Psa 75:3; Isa 24:20). The literal truth as to the earth is given (Job 26:7).

data.verse_text: He commands the sun not to shine; He seals off the stars.
data.commentary_text:
The sun, at His command, does not rise; namely, in an eclipse, or the darkness that accompanies earthquakes (Job 9:6). 

sealeth up the stars--that is, totally covers as one would seal up a room, that its contents may not be seen.

data.verse_text: He alone stretches out the heavens and treads on the waves of the sea.
data.commentary_text: spreadeth out-- (Isa 40:22; Psa 104:2). But throughout it is not so much God's creating, as His governing, power over nature that is set forth. A storm seems a struggle between Nature and her Lord! Better, therefore, "Who boweth the heavens alone," without help of any other. God descends from the bowed-down heaven to the earth (Psa 18:9). The storm, wherein the clouds descend, suggests this image. In the descent of the vault of heaven, God has come down from His high throne and walks majestically over the mountain waves (Hebrew, "heights"), as a conqueror taming their violence. So "tread upon" (Deu 33:29; Amo 4:13; Mat 14:26). The Egyptian hieroglyphic for impossibility is a man walking on waves.

data.verse_text: He is the Maker of the Bear and Orion, of the Pleiades and the constellations of the south.
data.commentary_text:
maketh--rather, from the Arabic, "covereth up." This accords better with the context, which describes His boundless power as controller rather than as creator [UMBREIT]. 

Arcturus--the great bear, which always revolves about the pole, and never sets. The Chaldeans and Arabs, early named the stars and grouped them in constellations; often travelling and tending flocks by night, they would naturally do so, especially as the rise and setting of some stars mark the distinction of seasons. BRINKLEY, presuming the stars here mentioned to be those of Taurus and Scorpio, and that these were the cardinal constellations of spring and autumn in Job's time, calculates, by the precession of equinoxes, the time of Job to be eight hundred eighteen years after the deluge, and one hundred eighty-four before Abraham. 

Orion--Hebrew, "the fool"; in Job 38:31 he appears fettered with "bands." The old legend represented this star as a hero, who presumptuously rebelled against God, and was therefore a fool, and was chained in the sky as a punishment; for its rising is at the stormy period of the year. He is Nimrod (the exceedingly impious rebel) among the Assyrians; Orion among the Greeks. Sabaism (worship of the heavenly hosts) and hero-worship were blended in his person. He first subverted the patriarchal order of society by substituting a chieftainship based on conquest (Gen 10:9-10). 

Pleiades--literally, "the heap of stars"; Arabic, "knot of stars." The various names of this constellation in the East express the close union of the stars in it (Amo 5:8). 

chambers of the south--the unseen regions of the southern hemisphere, with its own set of stars, as distinguished from those just mentioned of the northern. The true structure of the earth is here implied.

data.verse_text: He does great things beyond searching out, and wonders without number.
data.commentary_text: Repeated from Eliphaz (Job 5:9).

data.verse_text: Were He to pass by me, I would not see Him; were He to move, I would not recognize Him.
data.commentary_text: I see him not: he passeth on--The image is that of a howling wind (Isa 21:1). Like it when it bursts invisibly upon man, so God is felt in the awful effects of His wrath, but is not seen (Joh 3:8). Therefore, reasons Job, it is impossible to contend with Him.

data.verse_text: If He takes away, who can stop Him? Who dares to ask Him, ‘What are You doing?’
data.commentary_text: If "He taketh away," as in my case all that was dear to me, still a mortal cannot call Him to account. He only takes His own. He is an absolute King (Ecc 8:4; Dan 4:35).

data.verse_text: God does not restrain His anger; the helpers of Rahab cower beneath Him.
data.commentary_text:
If God--rather, "God will not withdraw His anger," that is, so long as a mortal obstinately resists [UMBREIT]. 

the proud helpers--The arrogant, who would help one contending with the Almighty, are of no avail against Him.

data.verse_text: How then can I answer Him or choose my arguments against Him?
data.commentary_text: How much less shall I? &c.--who am weak, seeing that the mighty have to stoop before Him. Choose words (use a well-chosen speech, in order to reason) with Him.

data.verse_text: For even if I were right, I could not answer. I could only beg my Judge for mercy.
data.commentary_text: (Job 10:15). Though I were conscious of no sin, yet I would not dare to say so, but leave it to His judgment and mercy to justify me (Co1 4:4).

data.verse_text: If I summoned Him and He answered me, I do not believe He would listen to my voice. For He would crush me with a tempest and multiply my wounds without cause. He does not let me catch my breath, but overwhelms me with bitterness.
data.commentary_text: would I not believe that he had hearkened unto my voice--who breaketh me (as a tree stripped of its leaves) with a tempest.

data.verse_text: If it is a matter of strength, He is indeed mighty! If it is a matter of justice, who can summon Him ?
data.commentary_text: UMBREIT takes these as the words of God, translating, "What availeth the might of the strong?" "Here (saith he) behold! what availeth justice? Who will appoint me a time to plead?" (So Jer 49:19). The last words certainly apply better to God than to Job. The sense is substantially the same if we make "me" apply to Job. The "lo!" expresses God's swift readiness for battle when challenged.

data.verse_text: Even if I were righteous, my mouth would condemn me; if I were blameless, it would declare me guilty.
data.commentary_text: it-- (Job 15:6; Luk 19:22); or "He," God.

data.verse_text: Though I am blameless, I have no concern for myself; I despise my own life.
data.commentary_text: Literally, here (and in Job 9:20), "I perfect! I should not know my soul! I would despise," [that is], "disown my life"; that is, Though conscious of innocence, I should be compelled, in contending with the infinite God, to ignore my own soul and despise my past life as if it were guilty [ROSENMULLER].

data.verse_text: It is all the same, and so I say, ‘He destroys both the blameless and the wicked.’
data.commentary_text: one thing--"It is all one; whether perfect or wicked--He destroyeth." This was the point Job maintained against his friends, that the righteous and wicked alike are afflicted, and that great sufferings here do not prove great guilt (Luk 13:1-5; Ecc 9:2).

data.verse_text: When the scourge brings sudden death, He mocks the despair of the innocent.
data.commentary_text: If--Rather, "While (His) scourge slays suddenly (the wicked, Job 9:22), He laughs at (disregards; not derides) the pining away of the innocent." The only difference, says Job, between the innocent and guilty is, the latter are slain by a sudden stroke, the former pine away gradually. The translation, "trial," does not express the antithesis to "slay suddenly," as "pining away" does [UMBREIT].

data.verse_text: The earth is given into the hand of the wicked; He blindfolds its judges. If it is not He, then who is it?
data.commentary_text:
Referring to righteous "judges," in antithesis to "the wicked" in the parallel first clause, whereas the wicked oppressor often has the earth given into his hand, the righteous judges are led to execution--culprits had their faces covered preparatory to execution (Est 7:8). Thus the contrast of the wicked and righteous here answers to that in Job 9:23. 

if not, where and who?--If God be not the cause of these anomalies, where is the cause to be found, and who is he?

data.verse_text: My days are swifter than a runner; they flee without seeing good.
data.commentary_text: a post--a courier. In the wide Persian empire such couriers, on dromedaries or on foot, were employed to carry the royal commands to the distant provinces (Est 3:13, Est 3:15; Est 8:14). "My days" are not like the slow caravan, but the fleet post. The "days" are themselves poetically said to "see no good," instead of Job in them (Pe1 3:10).

data.verse_text: They sweep by like boats of papyrus, like an eagle swooping down on its prey. If I were to say, ‘I will forget my complaint and change my expression and smile,’
data.commentary_text: swift ships--rather, canoes of reeds or papyrus skiffs, used on the Nile, swift from their lightness (Isa 18:2).

data.verse_text: I would still dread all my sufferings; I know that You will not acquit me.
data.commentary_text: The apodosis to Job 9:27 --"If I say, &c." "I still am afraid of all my sorrows (returning), for I know that thou wilt (dost) (by removing my sufferings) not hold or declare me innocent. How then can I leave off my heaviness?"

data.verse_text: Since I am already found guilty, why should I labor in vain?
data.commentary_text: The "if" is better omitted; I (am treated by God as) wicked; why then labor I in vain (to disprove His charge)? Job submits, not so much because he is convinced that God is right, as because God is powerful and he weak [BARNES].

data.verse_text: If I should wash myself with snow and cleanse my hands with lye, then You would plunge me into the pit, and even my own clothes would despise me.
data.commentary_text:
snow water--thought to be more cleansing than common water, owing to the whiteness of snow (Psa 51:7; Isa 1:18). 

never so clean--Better, to answer to the parallelism of the first clause which expresses the cleansing material, "lye:" the Arabs used alkali mixed with oil, as soap (Psa 73:13; Jer 2:22).

data.verse_text: For He is not a man like me, that I can answer Him, that we can take each other to court.
Nor is there a mediator between us, to lay his hand upon us both.
data.commentary_text: daysman--"mediator," or "umpire"; the imposition of whose hand expresses power to adjudicate between the persons. There might be one on a level with Job, the one party; but Job knew of none on a level with the Almighty, the other party (Sa1 2:25). We Christians know of such a Mediator (not, however, in the sense of umpire) on a level with both--the God-man, Christ Jesus (Ti1 2:5).

data.verse_text: Let Him remove His rod from me, so that His terror will no longer frighten me.
data.commentary_text: rod--not here the symbol of punishment, but of power. Job cannot meet God on fair terms so long as God deals with him on the footing of His almighty power.

data.verse_text: Then I would speak without fear of Him. But as it is, I am on my own.
data.commentary_text:
it is not so with me--As it now is, God not taking His rod away, I am not on such a footing of equality as to be able to vindicate myself.




Next: Job Chapter 10

data.verse_text: “I loathe my own life; I will express my complaint and speak in the bitterness of my soul.
data.commentary_text:
JOB'S REPLY TO BILDAD CONTINUED. (Job 10:1-22)

leave my complaint upon myself--rather, "I will give loose to my complaint" (Job 7:11).

data.verse_text: I will say to God: Do not condemn me! Let me know why You prosecute me.
data.commentary_text: show me, &c.--Do not, by virtue of Thy mere sovereignty, treat me as guilty without showing me the reasons.

data.verse_text: Does it please You to oppress me, to reject the work of Your hands and favor the schemes of the wicked?
data.commentary_text:
Job is unwilling to think God can have pleasure in using His power to "oppress" the weak, and to treat man, the work of His own hands, as of no value (Job 10:8; Psa 138:8). 

shine upon--favor with prosperity (Psa 50:2).

data.verse_text: Do You have eyes of flesh? Do You see as man sees? Are Your days like those of a mortal, or Your years like those of a man, that You should seek my iniquity and search out my sin—
data.commentary_text: Dost Thou see as feebly as man? that is, with the same uncharitable eye, as, for instance, Job's friends? Is Thy time as short? Impossible! Yet one might think, from the rapid succession of Thy strokes, that Thou hadst no time to spare in overwhelming me.

data.verse_text: though You know that I am not guilty, and there is no deliverance from Your hand?
data.commentary_text:
"Although Thou (the Omniscient) knowest," &c. (connected with Job 10:6), "Thou searchest after my sin." 

and . . . that none that can deliver out of thine hand--Therefore Thou hast no need to deal with me with the rapid violence which man would use (see Job 10:6).

data.verse_text: Your hands shaped me and altogether formed me. Would You now turn and destroy me?
data.commentary_text:
Made--with pains; implying a work of difficulty and art; applying to God language applicable only to man. 

together round about--implying that the human body is a complete unity, the parts of which on all sides will bear the closest scrutiny.

data.verse_text: Please remember that You molded me like clay. Would You now return me to dust?
data.commentary_text: clay--Job 10:10 proves that the reference here is, not so much to the perishable nature of the materials, as to their wonderful fashioning by the divine potter.

data.verse_text: Did You not pour me out like milk, and curdle me like cheese?
data.commentary_text: In the organization of the body from its rude commencements, the original liquid gradually assumes a more solid consistency, like milk curdling into cheese (Psa 139:15-16). Science reveals that the chyle circulated by the lacteal vessels is the supply to every organ.

data.verse_text: You clothed me with skin and flesh, and knit me together with bones and sinews.
data.commentary_text: fenced--or "inlaid" (Psa 139:15); "curiously wrought" [UMBREIT]. In the fÅ“tus the skin appears first, then the flesh, then the harder parts.

data.verse_text: You have granted me life and loving devotion, and Your care has preserved my spirit.
data.commentary_text:
visitation--Thy watchful Providence. 

spirit--breath.

data.verse_text: Yet You concealed these things in Your heart, and I know that this was in Your mind:
data.commentary_text: is with thee--was Thy purpose. All God's dealings with Job in his creation, preservation, and present afflictions were part of His secret counsel (Psa 139:16; Act 15:18; Ecc 3:11).

data.verse_text: If I sinned, You would take note, and would not acquit me of my iniquity.
data.commentary_text: Job is perplexed because God "marks" every sin of his with such ceaseless rigor. Whether "wicked" (godless and a hypocrite) or "righteous" (comparatively sincere), God condemns and punishes alike.

data.verse_text: If I am guilty, woe to me! And even if I am righteous, I cannot lift my head. I am full of shame and aware of my affliction.
data.commentary_text:
lift up my head--in conscious innocence (Psa 3:3). 

see thou--rather, "and seeing I see (I too well see) mine affliction," (which seems to prove me guilty) [UMBREIT].

data.verse_text: Should I hold my head high, You would hunt me like a lion, and again display Your power against me.
data.commentary_text:
increaseth--rather, "(if) I lift up (my head) Thou wouldest hunt me," &c. [UMBREIT]. 

and again--as if a lion should not kill his prey at once, but come back and torture it again.

data.verse_text: You produce new witnesses against me and multiply Your anger toward me. Hardships assault me in wave after wave. Why then did You bring me from the womb? Oh, that I had died, and no eye had seen me! If only I had never come to be, but had been carried from the womb to the grave.
data.commentary_text:
witnesses--His accumulated trials were like a succession of witnesses brought up in proof of his guilt, to wear out the accused. 

changes and war--rather, "(thou settest in array) against me host after host" (literally, "changes and a host," that is, a succession of hosts); namely, his afflictions, and then reproach upon reproach from his friends.

data.verse_text: Are my days not few? Withdraw from me, that I may have a little comfort, before I go—never to return— to a land of darkness and gloom,
data.commentary_text: But, since I was destined from my birth to these ills, at least give me a little breathing time during the few days left me (Job 9:34; Job 13:21; Psa 39:13).

data.verse_text: to a land of utter darkness, of deep shadow and disorder, where even the light is like darkness.”
data.commentary_text:
The ideas of order and light, disorder and darkness, harmonize (Gen 1:2). Three Hebrew words are used for darkness; in Job 10:21 (1) the common word "darkness"; here (2) "a land of gloom" (from a Hebrew root, "to cover up"); (3) as "thick darkness" or blackness (from a root, expressing sunset). "Where the light thereof is like blackness." Its only sunshine is thick darkness. A bold figure of poetry. Job in a better frame has brighter thoughts of the unseen world. But his views at best wanted the definite clearness of the Christian's. Compare with his words here Rev 21:23; Rev 22:5; Ti2 1:10.




Next: Job Chapter 11

data.verse_text: Then Zophar the Naamathite replied: “Should this stream of words go unanswered and such a speaker be vindicated?
data.commentary_text:
FIRST SPEECH OF ZOPHAR. (Job 11:1-20)

Zophar assails Job for his empty words, and indirectly, the two friends, for their weak reply. Taciturnity is highly prized among Orientals (Pro 10:8, Pro 10:19).

data.verse_text: Should your babbling put others to silence? Will you scoff without rebuke?
data.commentary_text:
lies--rather, "vain boasting" (Isa 16:6; Jer 48:30). The "men" is emphatic; men of sense; in antithesis to "vain boasting." 

mockest--upbraidest God by complaints, "shall no man make thee ashamed?"

data.verse_text: You have said, ‘My doctrine is sound, and I am pure in Your sight.’ But if only God would speak and open His lips against you,
data.commentary_text:
doctrine--purposely used of Job's speeches, which sounded like lessons of doctrine (Deu 32:2; Pro 4:2). 

thine--addressed to God. Job had maintained his sincerity against his friends suspicions, not faultlessness.

data.verse_text: and disclose to you the secrets of wisdom, for true wisdom has two sides. Know then that God exacts from you less than your iniquity deserves.
data.commentary_text:
to that which is!--Rather, "they are double to [man's] wisdom" [MICHAELIS]. So the Hebrew is rendered (Pro 2:7). God's ways, which you arraign, if you were shown their secret wisdom, would be seen vastly to exceed that of men, including yours (Co1 1:25). 

exacteth--Rather, "God consigns to oblivion in thy favor much of thy guilt."

data.verse_text: Can you fathom the deep things of God or discover the limits of the Almighty?
data.commentary_text: Rather, "Penetrate to the perfections of the Almighty" (Job 9:10; Psa 139:6).

data.verse_text: They are higher than the heavens—what can you do? They are deeper than Sheol—what can you know? Their measure is longer than the earth and wider than the sea.
data.commentary_text:
It--the "wisdom" of God (Job 11:6). The abruptness of the Hebrew is forcible: "The heights of heaven! What canst thou do" (as to attaining to them with thy gaze, Psa 139:8)? 

know--namely, of His perfections.

data.verse_text: If He comes along to imprison you, or convenes a court, who can stop Him?
data.commentary_text:
cut off--Rather, as in Job 9:11, "pass over," as a storm; namely, rush upon in anger. 

shut up--in prison, with a view to trial. 

gather together--the parties for judgment: hold a judicial assembly; to pass sentence on the prisoners.

data.verse_text: Surely He knows the deceit of men. If He sees iniquity, does He not take note?
data.commentary_text:
(Psa 94:11). 

consider--so as to punish it. Rather, from the connection, Job 11:6, "He seeth wickedness also, which man does not perceive"; literally, "But no (other, save He) perceiveth it" [UMBREIT]. God's "wisdom" (Job 11:6), detects sin where Job's human eye cannot reach (Job 11:8), so as to see any.

data.verse_text: But a witless man can no more become wise than the colt of a wild donkey can be born a man!
data.commentary_text:
vain--hollow. 

would be--"wants to consider himself wise"; opposed to God's "wisdom" (see on Job 11:11); refuses to see sin, where God sees it (Rom 1:22). 

wild ass's colt--a proverb for untamed wildness (Job 39:5, Job 39:8; Jer 2:24; Gen 16:12; Hebrew, "a wild-ass man"). Man wishes to appear wisely obedient to his Lord, whereas he is, from his birth, unsubdued in spirit.

data.verse_text: As for you, if you direct your heart and lift up your hands to Him,
data.commentary_text: The apodosis to the "If" is at Job 11:15. The preparation of the heart is to be obtained (Pro 16:1) by stretching out the hands in prayer for it (Psa 10:17; Ch1 29:18).

data.verse_text: if you put away the iniquity in your hand, and allow no injustice to dwell in your tents,
data.commentary_text: Rather, "if thou wilt put far away the iniquity in thine hand" (as Zaccheus did, Luk 19:8). The apodosis or conclusion is at Job 11:15, "then shalt thou," &c.

data.verse_text: then indeed you will lift up your face without shame; you will stand firm and unafraid.
data.commentary_text:
Zophar refers to Job's own words (Job 10:15), "yet will I not lift up my head," even though righteous. Zophar declares, if Job will follow his advice, he may "lift up his face." 

spot-- (Deu 32:5). 

steadfast--literally, "run fast together," like metals which become firm and hard by fusion. The sinner on the contrary is wavering.

data.verse_text: For you will forget your misery, recalling it only as waters gone by.
data.commentary_text: Just as when the stream runs dry (Job 6:17), the danger threatened by its wild waves is forgotten (Isa 65:16) [UMBREIT].

data.verse_text: Your life will be brighter than noonday; its darkness will be like the morning.
data.commentary_text:
age--days of life. 

the noon-day--namely, of thy former prosperity; which, in the poet's image, had gone on increasing, until it reached its height, as the sun rises higher and higher until it reaches the meridian (Pro 4:18). 

shine forth--rather, "though now in darkness, thou shall be as the morning"; or, "thy darkness (if any dark shade should arise on thee, it) shall be as the morning" (only the dullness of morning twilight, not nocturnal darkness) [UMBREIT].

data.verse_text: You will be secure, because there is hope, and you will look around and lie down in safety.
data.commentary_text:
The experience of thy life will teach thee there is hope for man in every trial. 

dig--namely, wells; the chief necessity in the East. Better, "though now ashamed (Rom 5:5, opposed to the previous 'hope'), thou shalt then rest safely" [GESENIUS];

data.verse_text: You will lie down without fear, and many will court your favor.
data.commentary_text:
(Psa 4:8; Pro 3:24; Isa 14:30); oriental images of prosperity. 

make suit--literally, "stroke thy face," "caress thee" (Pro 19:6).

data.verse_text: But the eyes of the wicked will fail, and escape will elude them; they will hope for their last breath.”
data.commentary_text:
A warning to Job, if he would not turn to God. 

The wicked--that is, obdurate sinners. 

eyes . . . fail--that is, in vain look for relief (Deu 28:65). Zophar implies Job's only hope of relief is in a change of heart. 

they shall not escape--literally, "every refuge shall vanish from them." 

giving up of the ghost--Their hope shall leave them as the breath does the body (Pro 11:7).




Next: Job Chapter 12

data.verse_text: Then Job answered: “Truly then you are the people with whom wisdom itself will die!
data.commentary_text:
JOB'S REPLY TO ZOPHAR (Job 12:1-14:22)

wisdom shall die with you--Ironical, as if all the wisdom in the world was concentrated in them and would expire when they expired. Wisdom makes "a people:" a foolish nation is "not a people" (Rom 10:19).

data.verse_text: But I also have a mind; I am not inferior to you. Who does not know such things as these?
data.commentary_text:
not inferior--not vanquished in argument and "wisdom" (Job 13:2). 

such things as these--such commonplace maxims as you so pompously adduce.

data.verse_text: I am a laughingstock to my friends, though I called on God, and He answered. The righteous and upright man is a laughingstock.
data.commentary_text:
The unfounded accusations of Job's friends were a "mockery" of him. He alludes to Zophar's word, "mockest" (Job 11:3). 

neighbour, who calleth, &c.--rather, "I who call upon God that he may answer me favorably" [UMBREIT].

data.verse_text: The one at ease scorns misfortune as the fate of those whose feet are slipping.
data.commentary_text: Rather, "a torch" (lamp) is an object of contempt in the thoughts of him who rests securely (is at ease), though it was prepared for the falterings of the feet [UMBREIT] (Pro 25:19). "Thoughts" and "feet" are in contrast; also rests "securely," and "falterings." The wanderer, arrived at his night-quarters, contemptuously throws aside the torch which had guided his uncertain steps through the darkness. As the torch is to the wanderer, so Job to his friends. Once they gladly used his aid in their need; now they in prosperity mock him in his need.

data.verse_text: The tents of robbers are safe, and those who provoke God are secure— those who carry their god in their hands.
data.commentary_text:
Job shows that the matter of fact opposes Zophar's theory (Job 11:14, Job 11:19-20) that wickedness causes insecurity in men's "tabernacles." On the contrary, they who rob the "tabernacles" ("dwellings") of others "prosper securely" in their own. 

into whose hand, &c.--rather, "who make a god of their own hand," that is, who regard their might as their only ruling principle [UMBREIT].

data.verse_text: But ask the animals, and they will instruct you; ask the birds of the air, and they will tell you.
data.commentary_text: Beasts, birds, fishes, and plants, reasons Job, teach that the violent live the most securely (Job 12:6). The vulture lives more securely than the dove, the lion than the ox, the shark than the dolphin, the rose than the thorn which tears it.

data.verse_text: Or speak to the earth, and it will teach you; let the fish of the sea inform you.
data.commentary_text: speak to the earth--rather, "the shrubs of the earth" [UMBREIT].

data.verse_text: Which of all these does not know that the hand of the LORD has done this?
data.commentary_text: In all these cases, says Job, the agency must be referred to Jehovah, though they may seem to man to imply imperfection (Job 12:6; Job 9:24). This is the only undisputed passage of the poetical part in which the name "Jehovah" occurs; in the historical parts it occurs frequently.

data.verse_text: The life of every living thing is in His hand, as well as the breath of all mankind.
data.commentary_text: the soul--that is, the animal life. Man, reasons Job, is subjected to the same laws as the lower animals.

data.verse_text: Does not the ear test words as the tongue tastes its food?
data.commentary_text: As the mouth by tasting meats selects what pleases it, so the ear tries the words of others and retains what is convincing. Each chooses according to his taste. The connection with Job 12:12 is in reference to Bildad's appeal to the "ancients" (Job 8:8). You are right in appealing to them, since "with them was wisdom," &c. But you select such proverbs of theirs as suit your views; so I may borrow from the same such as suit mine.

data.verse_text: Wisdom is found with the elderly, and understanding comes with long life.
data.commentary_text: ancient--aged (Job 15:10).

data.verse_text: Wisdom and strength belong to God; counsel and understanding are His.
data.commentary_text: In contrast to, "with the ancient is wisdom" (Job 12:12), Job quotes a saying of the ancients which suits his argument, "with Him (God) is (the true) wisdom" (Pro 8:14); and by that "wisdom and strength" "He breaketh down," &c., as an absolute Sovereign, not allowing man to penetrate His mysteries; man's part is to bow to His unchangeable decrees (Job 1:21). The Mohammedan saying is, "if God will, and how God will."

data.verse_text: What He tears down cannot be rebuilt; the man He imprisons cannot be released.
data.commentary_text: shutteth up-- (Isa 22:22). Job refers to Zophar's "shut up" (Job 11:10).

data.verse_text: If He holds back the waters, they dry up, and if He releases them, they overwhelm the land.
data.commentary_text: Probably alluding to the flood.

data.verse_text: True wisdom and power belong to Him. The deceived and the deceiver are His. He leads counselors away barefoot and makes fools of judges.
He loosens the bonds placed by kings and fastens a belt around their waists.
data.commentary_text:
He looseth the bond of kings--He looseth the authority of kings--the "bond" with which they bind their subjects (Isa 45:1; Gen 14:4; Dan 2:21). 

a girdle--the cord, with which they are bound as captives, instead of the royal "girdle" they once wore (Isa 22:21), and the bond they once bound others with. So "gird"--put on one the bonds of a prisoner instead of the ordinary girdle (Joh 21:18).

data.verse_text: He leads priests away barefoot and overthrows the established.
data.commentary_text:
princes--rather, "priests," as the Hebrew is rendered (Psa 99:6). Even the sacred ministers of religion are not exempt from reverses and captivity. 

the mighty--rather, "the firm-rooted in power"; the Arabic root expresses ever-flowing water [UMBREIT].

data.verse_text: He deprives the trusted of speech and takes away the discernment of elders.
data.commentary_text:
the trusty--rather, "those secure in their eloquence"; for example, the speakers in the gate (Isa 3:3) [BEZA]. 

understanding--literally, "taste," that is, insight or spiritual discernment, which experience gives the aged. The same Hebrew word is applied to Daniel's wisdom in interpretation (Dan 2:14).

data.verse_text: He pours out contempt on nobles and disarms the mighty.
data.commentary_text:
job 12:21Psa 107:40 quotes, in its first clause, this verse and, in its second, Job 12:24. 

weakeneth the strength--literally, "looseth the girdle"; Orientals wear flowing garments; when active strength is to be put forth, they gird up their garments with a girdle. Hence here--"He destroyeth their power" in the eyes of the people.

data.verse_text: He reveals the deep things of darkness and brings deep shadows into light.
He makes nations great and destroys them; He enlarges nations, then disperses them.
data.commentary_text:
job 12:23Isa 9:3; Psa 107:38-39, which Psalm quotes this chapter elsewhere. (See on Job 12:21). 

straiteneth--literally, "leadeth in," that is, "reduces."

data.verse_text: He deprives the earth’s leaders of reason and makes them wander in a trackless wasteland.
data.commentary_text:
heart--intelligence. 

wander in a wilderness--figurative; not referring to any actual fact. This cannot be quoted to prove Job lived after Israel's wanderings in the desert. Psa 107:4, Psa 107:40 quotes this passage.

data.verse_text: They grope in the darkness without light; He makes them stagger like drunkards.
data.commentary_text:
job 12:25Deu 28:29; Psa 107:27 again quote Job, but in a different connection.




Next: Job Chapter 13

data.verse_text: “Indeed, my eyes have seen all this; my ears have heard and understood. What you know, I also know; I am not inferior to you.
data.commentary_text:
JOB'S REPLY TO ZOPHAR CONTINUED. (Job 13:1-28)

all this--as to the dealings of Providence (Job 12:3).

data.verse_text: Yet I desire to speak to the Almighty and argue my case before God.
data.commentary_text: Job wishes to plead his cause before God (Job 9:34-35), as he is more and more convinced of the valueless character of his would-be "physicians" (Job 16:2).

data.verse_text: You, however, smear with lies; you are all worthless physicians.
data.commentary_text: forgers of lies--literally, "artful twisters of vain speeches" [UMBREIT].

data.verse_text: If only you would remain silent; for that would be your wisdom! Hear now my argument, and listen to the plea of my lips.
data.commentary_text: (Pro 17:28). The Arabs say, "The wise are dumb; silence is wisdom."

data.verse_text: Will you speak wickedly on God’s behalf or speak deceitfully for Him?
data.commentary_text: deceitfully--use fallacies to vindicate God in His dealings; as if the end justified the means. Their "deceitfulness" for God, against Job, was that they asserted he was a sinner, because he was a sufferer.

data.verse_text: Would you show Him partiality or argue in His defense?
data.commentary_text:
accept his person--God's; that is, be partial for Him, as when a judge favors one party in a trial, because of personal considerations. 

contend for God--namely, with fallacies and prepossessions against Job before judgment (Jdg 6:31). Partiality can never please the impartial God, nor the goodness of the cause excuse the unfairness of the arguments.

data.verse_text: Would it be well when He examined you? Could you deceive Him like a man?
data.commentary_text:
Will the issue to you be good, when He searches out you and your arguments? Will you be regarded by Him as pure and disinterested? 

mock-- (Gal 6:7). Rather, "Can you deceive Him as one man?" &c.

data.verse_text: Surely He would rebuke you if you secretly showed partiality.
data.commentary_text: If ye do, though secretly, act partially. (See on Job 13:8; Psa 82:1-2). God can successfully vindicate His acts, and needs no fallacious argument of man.

data.verse_text: Would His majesty not terrify you? Would the dread of Him not fall upon you?
data.commentary_text: make you afraid?--namely, of employing sophisms in His name (Jer 10:7, Jer 10:10).

data.verse_text: Your maxims are proverbs of ashes; your defenses are defenses of clay.
data.commentary_text:
remembrances--"proverbial maxims," so called because well remembered. 

like unto ashes--or, "parables of ashes"; the image of lightness and nothingness (Isa 44:20). 

bodies--rather, "entrenchments"; those of clay, as opposed to those of stone, are easy to be destroyed; so the proverbs, behind which they entrench themselves, will not shelter them when God shall appear to reprove them for their injustice to Job.

data.verse_text: Be silent, and I will speak. Then let come to me what may.
data.commentary_text: Job would wish to be spared their speeches, so as to speak out all his mind as to his wretchedness (Job 13:14), happen what will.

data.verse_text: Why do I put myself at risk and take my life in my own hands?
data.commentary_text: A proverb for, "Why should I anxiously desire to save my life?" [EICHORN]. The image in the first clause is that of a wild beast, which in order to preserve his prey, carries it in his teeth. That in the second refers to men who hold in the hand what they want to keep secure.

data.verse_text: Though He slay me, I will hope in Him. I will still defend my ways to His face.
data.commentary_text: in him--So the margin or keri, reads. But the textual reading or chetib is "not," which agrees best with the context, and other passages wherein he says he has no hope (Job 6:11; Job 7:21; Job 10:20; Job 19:10). "Though He slay me, and I dare no more hope, yet I will maintain," &c., that is, "I desire to vindicate myself before Him," as not a hypocrite [UMBREIT and NOYES].

data.verse_text: Moreover, this will be my salvation, for no godless man can appear before Him.
data.commentary_text: He--rather, "This also already speaks in my behalf (literally, 'for my saving acquittal') for an hypocrite would not wish to come before Him" (as I do) [UMBREIT]. (See last clause of Job 13:15).

data.verse_text: Listen carefully to my words; let my declaration ring in your ears.
data.commentary_text:
my declaration--namely, that I wish to be permitted to justify myself immediately before God. 

with your ears--that is, attentively.

data.verse_text: Behold, now that I have prepared my case, I know that I will be vindicated.
data.commentary_text: ordered--implying a constant preparation for defense in his confidence of innocence.

data.verse_text: Can anyone indict me? If so, I will be silent and die.
data.commentary_text: if, &c.--Rather, "Then would I hold my tongue and give up the ghost"; that is, if any one can contend with me and prove me false, I have no more to say. "I will be silent and die." Like our "I would stake my life on it" [UMBREIT].

data.verse_text: Only grant these two things to me, so that I need not hide from You:
data.commentary_text:
Address to God. 

not hide--stand forth boldly to maintain my cause.

data.verse_text: Withdraw Your hand from me, and do not let Your terror frighten me.
data.commentary_text: (See on Job 9:34 and see Psa 39:10).

data.verse_text: Then call me, and I will answer, or let me speak, and You can reply.
data.commentary_text:
call--a challenge to the defendant to answer to the charges. 

answer--the defense begun. 

speak--as plaintiff. 

answer--to the plea of the plaintiff. Expressions from a trial.

data.verse_text: How many are my iniquities and sins? Reveal to me my transgression and sin.
data.commentary_text:
The catalogue of my sins ought to be great, to judge from the severity with which God ever anew crushes one already bowed down. Would that He would reckon them up! He then would see how much my calamities outnumber them. 

sin?--singular, "I am unconscious of a single particular sin, much less many" [UMBREIT].

data.verse_text: Why do You hide Your face and consider me as Your enemy?
data.commentary_text:
hidest . . . face--a figure from the gloomy impression caused by the sudden clouding over of the sun. 

enemy--God treated Job as an enemy who must be robbed of power by ceaseless sufferings (Job 7:17, Job 7:21).

data.verse_text: Would You frighten a windblown leaf? Would You chase after dry chaff?
data.commentary_text:
(Lev 26:36; Psa 1:4). Job compares himself to a leaf already fallen, which the storm still chases hither and thither. 

break--literally, "shake with (Thy) terrors." Jesus Christ does not "break the bruised reed" (Isa 42:3, Isa 27:8).

data.verse_text: For You record bitter accusations against me and bequeath to me the iniquities of my youth.
data.commentary_text:
writest--a judicial phrase, to note down the determined punishment. The sentence of the condemned used to be written down (Isa 10:1; Jer 22:30; Psa 149:9) [UMBREIT]. 

bitter things--bitter punishments. 

makest me to possess--or "inherit." In old age he receives possession of the inheritance of sin thoughtlessly acquired in youth. "To inherit sins" is to inherit the punishments inseparably connected with them in Hebrew ideas (Psa 25:7).

data.verse_text: You put my feet in the stocks and stand watch over all my paths; You set a limit for the soles of my feet.
data.commentary_text:
stocks--in which the prisoner's feet were made fast until the time of execution (Jer 20:2). 

lookest narrowly--as an overseer would watch a prisoner. 

print--Either the stocks, or his disease, marked his soles (Hebrew, "roots") as the bastinado would. Better, thou drawest (or diggest) [GESENIUS] a line (or trench) [GESENIUS] round my soles, beyond which I must not move [UMBREIT].

data.verse_text: So man wastes away like something rotten, like a moth-eaten garment.
data.commentary_text:
Job speaks of himself in the third person, thus forming the transition to the general lot of man (Job 14:1; Psa 39:11; Hos 5:12).




Next: Job Chapter 14

data.verse_text: “Man, who is born of woman, is short of days and full of trouble.
data.commentary_text:
JOB PASSES FROM HIS OWN TO THE COMMON MISERY OF MANKIND. (Job 14:1-22)

woman--feeble, and in the East looked down upon (Gen 2:21). Man being born of one so frail must be frail himself (Mat 11:11). 

few days-- (Gen 47:9; Psa 90:10). Literally, "short of days." Man is the reverse of full of days and short of trouble.

data.verse_text: Like a flower, he comes forth, then withers away; like a fleeting shadow, he does not endure.
data.commentary_text: (Psa 90:6; see on Job 8:9).

data.verse_text: Do You open Your eyes to one like this? Will You bring him into judgment before You?
data.commentary_text:
open . . . eyes upon--Not in graciousness; but, "Dost Thou sharply fix Thine eyes upon?" (See on Job 7:20; also see on Job 1:7). Is one so frail as man worthy of such constant watching on the part of God? (Zac 12:4). 

me--so frail. 

thee--so almighty.

data.verse_text: Who can bring out clean from unclean? No one!
data.commentary_text: A plea in mitigation. The doctrine of original sin was held from the first. "Man is unclean from his birth, how then can God expect perfect cleanness from such a one and deal so severely with me?"

data.verse_text: Since his days are determined and the number of his months is with You, and since You have set limits that he cannot exceed,
data.commentary_text: determined-- (Job 7:1; Isa 10:23; Dan 9:27; Dan 11:36).

data.verse_text: look away from him and let him rest, so he can enjoy his day as a hired hand.
data.commentary_text:
Turn--namely, Thine eyes from watching him so jealously (Job 14:3). 

hireling-- (Job 7:1). 

accomplish--rather, "enjoy." That he may at least enjoy the measure of rest of the hireling who though hard worked reconciles himself to his lot by the hope of his rest and reward [UMBREIT].

data.verse_text: For there is hope for a tree: If it is cut down, it will sprout again, and its tender shoots will not fail. If its roots grow old in the ground and its stump dies in the soil,
data.commentary_text: Man may the more claim a peaceful life, since, when separated from it by death, he never returns to it. This does not deny a future life, but a return to the present condition of life. Job plainly hopes for a future state (Job 14:13; Job 7:2). Still, it is but vague and trembling hope, not assurance; excepting the one bright glimpse in Job 19:25. The Gospel revelation was needed to change fears, hopes, and glimpses into clear and definite certainties.

data.verse_text: at the scent of water it will bud and put forth twigs like a sapling.
data.commentary_text:
scent--exhalation, which, rather than the humidity of water, causes the tree to germinate. In the antithesis to man the tree is personified, and volition is poetically ascribed to it. 

like a plant--"as if newly planted" [UMBREIT]; not as if trees and plants were a different species.

data.verse_text: But a man dies and is laid low; he breathes his last, and where is he?
data.commentary_text:
man . . . man--Two distinct Hebrew words are here used; Geber, a mighty man: though mighty, he dies. Adam, a man of earth: because earthly, he gives up the ghost. 

wasteth--is reduced to nothing: he cannot revive in the present state, as the tree does. The cypress and pine, which when cut down do not revive, were the symbols of death among the Romans.

data.verse_text: As water disappears from the sea and a river becomes parched and dry,
data.commentary_text:
sea--that is, a lake, or pool formed from the outspreading of a river. Job lived near the Euphrates: and "sea" is applied to it (Jer 51:36; Isa 27:1). So of the Nile (Isa 19:5). 

fail--utterly disappeared by drying up. The rugged channel of the once flowing water answers to the outstretched corpse ("lieth down," Job 14:12) of the once living man.

data.verse_text: so a man lies down and does not rise. Until the heavens are no more, he will not be awakened or roused from sleep.
data.commentary_text: heavens be no more--This only implies that Job had no hope of living again in the present order of the world, not that he had no hope of life again in a new order of things. Psa 102:26 proves that early under the Old Testament the dissolution of the present earth and heavens was expected (compare Gen 8:22). Enoch before Job had implied that the "saints shall live again" (Jde 1:14; Heb 11:13-16). Even if, by this phrase, Job meant "never" (Psa 89:29) in his gloomier state of feelings, yet the Holy Ghost has made him unconsciously (Pe1 1:11-12) use language expressing the truth, that the resurrection is to be preceded by the dissolution of the heavens. In Job 14:13-15 he plainly passes to brighter hopes of a world to come.

data.verse_text: If only You would hide me in Sheol and conceal me until Your anger has passed! If only You would appoint a time for me and then remember me!
data.commentary_text:
Job wishes to be kept hidden in the grave until God's wrath against him shall have passed away. So while God's wrath is visiting the earth for the abounding apostasy which is to precede the second coming, God's people shall be hidden against the resurrection glory (Isa 26:19-21). 

set time--a decreed time (Act 1:7).

data.verse_text: When a man dies, will he live again? All the days of my hard service I will wait, until my renewal comes.
data.commentary_text:
shall he live?--The answer implied is, There is a hope that he shall, though not in the present order of life, as is shown by the words following. Job had denied (Job 14:10-12) that man shall live again in this present world. But hoping for a "set time," when God shall remember and raise him out of the hiding-place of the grave (Job 14:13), he declares himself willing to "wait all the days of his appointed time" of continuance in the grave, however long and hard that may be. 

appointed time--literally, "warfare, hard service"; imlying the hardship of being shut out from the realms of life, light, and God for the time he shall be in the grave (Job 7:1). 

change--my release, as a soldier at his post released from duty by the relieving guard (see on Job 10:17) [UMBREIT and GESENIUS], but elsewhere GESENIUS explains it, "renovation," as of plants in spring (Job 14:7), but this does not accord so well with the metaphor in "appointed time" or "warfare."

data.verse_text: You will call, and I will answer; You will desire the work of Your hands.
data.commentary_text:
namely, at the resurrection (Joh 5:28; Psa 17:15). 

have a desire to--literally, "become pale with anxious desire:" the same word is translated "sore longedst after" (Gen 31:30; Psa 84:2), implying the utter unlikelihood that God would leave in oblivion the "creature of His own hands so fearfully and wonderfully made." It is objected that if Job knew of a future retribution, he would make it the leading topic in solving the problem of the permitted afflictions of the righteous. But, (1) He did not intend to exceed the limits of what was clearly revealed; the doctrine was then in a vague form only; (2) The doctrine of God's moral government in this life, even independently of the future, needed vindication.

data.verse_text: For then You would count my steps, but would not keep track of my sin.
data.commentary_text:
Rather, "Yea, thou wilt number my steps, and wilt not (as now) jealously watch over my sin." Thenceforward, instead of severe watching for every sin of Job, God will guard him against every sin. 

number . . . steps--that is, minutely attend to them, that they may not wander [UMBREIT] (Sa1 2:9; Psa 37:23).

data.verse_text: My transgression would be sealed in a bag, and You would cover over my iniquity.
data.commentary_text:
sealed up-- (Job 9:7). Is shut up in eternal oblivion, that is, God thenceforth will think no more of my former sins. To cover sins is to completely forgive them (Psa 32:1; Psa 85:2). Purses of money in the East are usually sealed. 

sewest up--rather, "coverest"; akin to an Arabic word, "to color over," to forget wholly.

data.verse_text: But as a mountain erodes and crumbles and a rock is dislodged from its place,
data.commentary_text:
cometh to naught--literally, "fadeth"; a poetical image from a leaf (Isa 34:4). Here Job falls back into his gloomy bodings as to the grave. Instead of "and surely," translate "yet"; marking the transition from his brighter hopes. Even the solid mountain falls and crumbles away; man therefore cannot "hope" to escape decay or to live again in the present world (Job 14:19). 

out of his place--so man (Psa 103:16).

data.verse_text: as water wears away the stones and torrents wash away the soil, so You destroy a man’s hope.
data.commentary_text:
The Hebrew order is more forcible: "Stones themselves are worn away by water." 

things which grow out of--rather, "floods wash away the dust of the earth." There is a gradation from "mountains" to "rocks" (Job 14:18), then "stones," then last "dust of the earth"; thus the solid mountain at last disappears utterly.

data.verse_text: You forever overpower him, and he passes on; You change his countenance and send him away.
data.commentary_text:
prevailest--dost overpower by superior strength. 

passeth--dieth. 

changest countenance--the change in the visage at death. Differently (Dan 5:9).

data.verse_text: If his sons receive honor, he does not know it; if they are brought low, he is unaware.
data.commentary_text: One striking trait is selected from the sad picture of the severance of the dead from all that passes in the world (Ecc 9:5), namely, the utter separation of parents and children.

data.verse_text: He feels only the pain of his own body and mourns only for himself.”
data.commentary_text:
"Flesh" and "soul" describe the whole man. Scripture rests the hope of a future life, not on the inherent immortality of the soul, but on the restoration of the body with the soul. In the unseen world, Job in a gloomy frame anticipates, man shall be limited to the thought of his own misery. "Pain is by personification, from our feelings while alive, attributed to the flesh and soul, as if the man could feel in his body when dead. It is the dead in general, not the wicked, who are meant here."




Next: Job Chapter 15

data.verse_text: Then Eliphaz the Temanite replied: “Does a wise man answer with empty counsel or fill his belly with the hot east wind? Should he argue with useless words or speeches that serve no purpose?
data.commentary_text:
SECOND SPEECH OF ELIPHAZ. (Job 15:1-35)

a wise man--which Job claims to be. 

vain knowledge--Hebrew, "windy knowledge"; literally, "of wind" (Job 8:2). In Ecc 1:14, Hebrew, "to catch wind," expresses to strive for what is vain. 

east wind--stronger than the previous "wind," for in that region the east wind is the most destructive of winds (Isa 27:8). Thus here,--empty violence. 

belly--the inward parts, the breast (Pro 18:8).

data.verse_text: But you even undermine the fear of God and hinder meditation before Him.
data.commentary_text:
fear--reverence for God (Job 4:6; Psa 2:11). 

prayer--meditation, in Psa 104:34; so devotion. If thy views were right, reasons Eliphaz, that God disregards the afflictions of the righteous and makes the wicked to prosper, all devotion would be at an end.

data.verse_text: For your iniquity instructs your mouth, and you choose the language of the crafty.
data.commentary_text: The sophistry of thine own speeches proves thy guilt.

data.verse_text: Your own mouth, not mine, condemns you; your own lips testify against you.
data.commentary_text: No pious man would utter such sentiments.

data.verse_text: Were you the first man ever born? Were you brought forth before the hills?
data.commentary_text: That is, Art thou wisdom personified? Wisdom existed before the hills; that is, the eternal Son of God (Pro 8:25; Psa 90:2). Wast thou in existence before Adam? The farther back one existed, the nearer he was to the Eternal Wisdom.

data.verse_text: Do you listen in on the council of God or limit wisdom to yourself?
data.commentary_text:
secret--rather, "Wast thou a listener in the secret council of God?" The Hebrew means properly the cushions of a divan on which counsellors in the East usually sit. God's servants are admitted to God's secrets (Psa 25:14; Gen 18:17; Joh 15:15). 

restrain--Rather, didst thou take away, or borrow, thence (namely, from the divine secret council) thy wisdom? Eliphaz in this (Job 15:8-9) retorts Job's words upon himself (Job 12:2-3; Job 13:2).

data.verse_text: What do you know that we do not? What do you understand that is not clear to us?
data.commentary_text: in us--or, "with us," Hebraism for "we are aware of."

data.verse_text: Both the gray-haired and the aged are on our side— men much older than your father.
data.commentary_text: On our side, thinking with us are the aged. Job had admitted that wisdom is with them (Job 12:12). Eliphaz seems to have been himself older than Job; perhaps the other two were also (Job 32:6). Job, in Job 30:1, does not refer to his three friends; it therefore forms no objection. The Arabs are proud of fulness of years.

data.verse_text: Are the consolations of God not enough for you, even words spoken gently to you?
data.commentary_text:
consolations--namely, the revelation which Eliphaz had stated as a consolatory reproof to Job, and which he repeats in Job 15:14. 

secret--Hast thou some secret wisdom and source of consolation, which makes thee disregard those suggested by me? (Job 15:8). Rather, from a different Hebrew root, Is the word of kindness or gentleness addressed by me treated by thee as valueless? [UMBREIT].

data.verse_text: Why has your heart carried you away, and why do your eyes flash,
data.commentary_text: wink--that is, why do thy eyes evince pride? (Pro 6:13; Psa 35:19).

data.verse_text: as you turn your spirit against God and pour such words from your mouth?
data.commentary_text: That is, frettest against God and lettest fall rash words.

data.verse_text: What is man, that he should be pure, or one born of woman, that he should be righteous?
data.commentary_text: Eliphaz repeats the revelation (Job 4:17) in substance, but using Job's own words (see on Job 14:1, on "born of a woman") to strike him with his own weapons.

data.verse_text: If God puts no trust in His holy ones, if even the heavens are not pure in His eyes,
data.commentary_text:
Repeated from Job 4:18; "servants" there are "saints" here; namely, holy angels. 

heavens--literally, or else answering to "angels" (see on Job 4:18, and Job 25:5).

data.verse_text: how much less man, who is vile and corrupt, who drinks injustice like water?
data.commentary_text:
filthy--in Arabic "sour" (Psa 14:3; Psa 53:3), corrupted from his original purity. 

drinketh-- (Pro 19:28).

data.verse_text: Listen to me and I will inform you. I will describe what I have seen,
data.commentary_text: In direct contradiction of Job's position (Job 12:6, &c.), that the lot of the wicked was the most prosperous here, Eliphaz appeals (1) to his own experience, (2) to the wisdom of the ancients.

data.verse_text: what was declared by wise men and was not concealed from their fathers,
data.commentary_text: Rather, "and which as handed down from their fathers, they have not concealed."

data.verse_text: to whom alone the land was given when no foreigner passed among them.
data.commentary_text: Eliphaz speaks like a genuine Arab when he boasts that his ancestors had ever possessed the land unmixed with foreigners [UMBREIT]. His words are intended to oppose Job's (Job 9:24); "the earth" in their case was not "given into the hand of the wicked." He refers to the division of the earth by divine appointment (Gen 10:5; Gen 25:32). Also he may insinuate that Job's sentiments had been corrupted from original purity by his vicinity to the Sabeans and Chaldeans [ROSENMULLER].

data.verse_text: A wicked man writhes in pain all his days; only a few years are reserved for the ruthless.
data.commentary_text:
travaileth--rather, "trembleth of himself," though there is no real danger [UMBREIT]. 

and the number of his years, &c.--This gives the reason why the wicked man trembles continually; namely, because he knows not the moment when his life must end.

data.verse_text: Sounds of terror fill his ears; in his prosperity the destroyer attacks him.
data.commentary_text: An evil conscience conceives alarm at every sudden sound, though it be in a time of peace ("prosperity"), when there is no real danger (Lev 26:36; Pro 28:1; Kg2 7:6).

data.verse_text: He despairs of his return from darkness; he is marked for the sword.
data.commentary_text:
darkness--namely, danger or calamity. Glancing at Job, who despaired of restoration: in contrast to good men when in darkness (Mic 7:8-9). 

waited for of--that is, He is destined for the sword [GESENIUS]. Rather (in the night of danger), "he looks anxiously towards the sword," as if every sword was drawn against him [UMBREIT].

data.verse_text: He wanders about as food for vultures; he knows the day of darkness is at hand.
data.commentary_text:
Wandereth in anxious search for bread. Famine in Old Testament depicts sore need (Isa 5:13). Contrast the pious man's lot (Job 5:20-22). 

knoweth--has the firm conviction. Contrast the same word applied to the pious (Job 5:24-25). 

ready at his hand--an Arabic phrase to denote a thing's complete readiness and full presence, as if in the hand.

data.verse_text: Distress and anguish terrify him, overwhelming him like a king poised to attack.
data.commentary_text: prevail--break upon him suddenly and terribly, as a king, &c. (Pro 6:11).

data.verse_text: For he has stretched out his hand against God and has vaunted himself against the Almighty,
data.commentary_text: stretcheth . . . hand--wielding the spear, as a bold rebel against God (Job 9:4; Isa 27:4).

data.verse_text: rushing headlong at Him with a thick, studded shield.
data.commentary_text:
on his neck--rather, "with outstretched neck," namely, that of the rebel [UMBREIT] (Psa 75:5). 

upon . . . bucklers--rather, "with--his (the rebel's, not God's) bucklers." The rebel and his fellows are depicted as joining shields together, to form a compact covering over their heads against the weapons hurled on them from a fortress [UMBREIT and GESENIUS].

data.verse_text: Though his face is covered with fat and his waistline bulges with flesh,
data.commentary_text:
The well-nourished body of the rebel is the sign of his prosperity. 

collops--masses of fat. He pampers and fattens himself with sensual indulgences; hence his rebellion against God (Deu 32:15; Sa1 2:29).

data.verse_text: he will dwell in ruined cities, in abandoned houses destined to become rubble.
data.commentary_text:
The class of wicked here described is that of robbers who plunder "cities," and seize on the houses of the banished citizens (Isa 13:20). Eliphaz chooses this class because Job had chosen the same (Job 12:6). 

heaps--of ruins.

data.verse_text: He will no longer be rich; his wealth will not endure. His possessions will not overspread the land.
data.commentary_text:
Rather, he shall not increase his riches; he has reached his highest point; his prosperity shall not continue. 

perfection--rather, "His acquired wealth--what he possesses--shall not be extended," &c.

data.verse_text: He will not escape from the darkness; the flame will wither his shoots, and the breath of God’s mouth will carry him away.
data.commentary_text:
depart--that is, escape (Job 15:22-23). 

branches--namely, his offspring (Job 1:18-19; Psa 37:35). 

dry up--The "flame" is the sultry wind in the East by which plants most full of sap are suddenly shrivelled. 

his mouth--that is, God's wrath (Isa 11:4).

data.verse_text: Let him not deceive himself with trust in emptiness, for emptiness will be his reward.
data.commentary_text:
Rather, "let him not trust in vanity or he will be deceived," &c. 

vanity--that which is unsubstantial. Sin is its own punishment (Pro 1:31; Jer 2:19).

data.verse_text: It will be paid in full before his time, and his branch will not flourish.
data.commentary_text:
Literally, "it (the tree to which he is compared, Job 15:30, or else his life) shall not be filled up in its time"; that is, "he shall be ended before his time." 

shall not be green--image from a withered tree; the childless extinction of the wicked.

data.verse_text: He will be like a vine stripped of its unripe grapes, like an olive tree that sheds its blossoms.
data.commentary_text: Images of incompleteness. The loss of the unripe grapes is poetically made the vine tree's own act, in order to express more pointedly that the sinner's ruin is the fruit of his own conduct (Isa 3:11; Jer 6:19).

data.verse_text: For the company of the godless will be barren, and fire will consume the tents of bribery.
data.commentary_text:
Rather, The binding together of the hypocrites (wicked) shall be fruitless [UMBREIT]. 

tabernacles of bribery--namely, dwellings of unjust judges, often reprobated in the Old Testament (Isa 1:23). The "fire of God" that consumed Job's possessions (Job 1:16) Eliphaz insinuates may have been on account of Job's bribery as an Arab sheik or emir.

data.verse_text: They conceive trouble and give birth to evil; their womb is pregnant with deceit.”
data.commentary_text:
Bitter irony, illustrating the "unfruitfulness" (Job 15:34) of the wicked. Their conceptions and birthgivings consist solely in mischief, &c. (Isa 33:11). 

prepareth--hatcheth.




Next: Job Chapter 16

data.verse_text: Then Job answered: “I have heard many things like these; miserable comforters are you all.
data.commentary_text:
JOB'S REPLY. (Job 16:1-22)

(Job 13:4).

data.verse_text: Is there no end to your long-winded speeches? What provokes you to continue testifying?
data.commentary_text:
"Words of wind," Hebrew. He retorts upon Eliphaz his reproach (Job 15:2). 

emboldeneth--literally, "What wearies you so that ye contradict?" that is, What have I said to provoke you? &c. [SCHUTTENS]. Or, as better accords with the first clause, "Wherefore do ye weary yourselves contradicting?" [UMBREIT].

data.verse_text: I could also speak like you if you were in my place; I could heap up words against you and shake my head at you.
data.commentary_text:
heap up--rather, "marshal together (an army of) words against you." 

shake . . . head--in mockery; it means nodding, rather than shaking; nodding is not with us, as in the East, a gesture of scorn (Isa 37:22; Jer 18:16; Mat 27:39).

data.verse_text: But I would encourage you with my mouth, and the consolation of my lips would bring relief.
data.commentary_text: strengthen . . . with . . . mouth--bitter irony. In allusion to Eliphaz' boasted "consolations" (Job 15:11). Opposed to strengthening with the heart, that is, with real consolation. Translate, "I also (like you) could strengthen with the mouth," that is, with heartless talk: "And the moving of my lips (mere lip comfort) could console (in the same fashion as you do)" [UMBREIT]. "Hearty counsel" (Pro 27:9) is the opposite.

data.verse_text: Even if I speak, my pain is not relieved, and if I hold back, how will it go away?
data.commentary_text: eased--literally, "What (portion of my sufferings) goes from me?"

data.verse_text: Surely He has now exhausted me; You have devastated all my family.
data.commentary_text:
But now--rather, "ah!" 

he--God. 

company--rather, "band of witnesses," namely, those who could attest his innocence (his children, servants, &c.). So the same Hebrew is translated in Job 16:8. UMBREIT makes his "band of witnesses," himself, for, alas! he had no other witness for him. But this is too recondite.

data.verse_text: You have bound me, and it has become a witness; my frailty rises up and testifies against me.
data.commentary_text:
filled . . . with wrinkles--Rather (as also the same Hebrew word in Job 22:16; English Version, "cut down"), "thou hast fettered me, thy witness" (besides cutting off my "band of witnesses," Job 16:7), that is, hast disabled me by pains from properly attesting my innocence. But another "witness" arises against him, namely, his "leanness" or wretched state of body, construed by his friends into a proof of his guilt. The radical meaning of the Hebrew is "to draw together," whence flow the double meaning "to bind" or "fetter," and in Syriac, "to wrinkle." 

leanness--meaning also "lie"; implying it was a "false witness."

data.verse_text: His anger has torn me and opposed me; He gnashes His teeth at me. My adversary pierces me with His eyes.
data.commentary_text:
Image from a wild beast. So God is represented (Job 10:16). 

who hateth me--rather, "and pursues me hard." Job would not ascribe "hatred" to God (Psa 50:22). 

mine enemy--rather, "he sharpens, &c., as an enemy" (Psa 7:12). Darts wrathful glances at me, like a foe (Job 13:24).

data.verse_text: They open their mouths against me and strike my cheeks with contempt; they join together against me.
data.commentary_text:
gaped--not in order to devour, but to mock him. To fill his cup of misery, the mockery of his friends (Job 16:10) is added to the hostile treatment from God (Job 16:9). 

smitten . . . cheek--figurative for contemptuous abuse (Lam 3:30; Mat 5:39). 

gathered themselves--"conspired unanimously" [SCHUTTENS].

data.verse_text: God has delivered me to unjust men; He has thrown me to the clutches of the wicked.
data.commentary_text:
the ungodly--namely, his professed friends, who persecuted him with unkind speeches. 

turned me over--literally, "cast me headlong into the hands of the wicked."

data.verse_text: I was at ease, but He shattered me; He seized me by the neck and crushed me. He has set me up as His target;
data.commentary_text:
I was at ease--in past times (Job 1:1-3). 

by my neck--as an animal does its prey (so Job 10:16). 

shaken--violently; in contrast to his former "ease" (Psa 102:10). Set me up (again). 

mark-- (Job 7:20; Lam 3:12). God lets me always recover strength, so as to torment me ceaselessly.

data.verse_text: His archers surround me. He pierces my kidneys without mercy and spills my gall on the ground.
data.commentary_text:
his archers--The image of Job 16:12 is continued. God, in making me His "mark," is accompanied by the three friends, whose words wound like sharp arrows. 

gall--put for a vital part; so the liver (Lam 2:11).

data.verse_text: He breaks me with wound upon wound; He rushes me like a mighty warrior.
data.commentary_text:
The image is from storming a fortress by making breaches in the walls (Kg2 14:13). 

a giant--a mighty warrior.

data.verse_text: I have sewn sackcloth over my skin; I have buried my horn in the dust.
data.commentary_text:
sewed--denoting the tight fit of the mourning garment; it was a sack with armholes closely sewed to the body. 

horn--image from horned cattle, which when excited tear the earth with their horns. The horn was the emblem of power (Kg1 22:11). Here, it is 

in the dust--which as applied to Job denotes his humiliation from former greatness. To throw one's self in the dust was a sign of mourning; this idea is here joined with that of excited despair, depicted by the fury of a horned beast. The Druses of Lebanon still wear horns as an ornament.

data.verse_text: My face is red with weeping, and deep shadows ring my eyes; yet my hands are free of violence and my prayer is pure.
data.commentary_text:
foul--rather, "is red," that is, flushed and heated [UMBREIT and NOYES]. 

shadow of death--that is, darkening through many tears (Lam 5:17). Job here refers to Zophar's implied charge (Job 11:14). Nearly the same words occur as to Jesus Christ (Isa 53:9). So Job 16:10 above answers to the description of Jesus Christ (Psa 22:13; Isa 50:6, and Job 16:4 to Psa 22:7). He alone realized what Job aspired after, namely, outward righteousness of acts and inward purity of devotion. Jesus Christ as the representative man is typified in some degree in every servant of God in the Old Testament.

data.verse_text: O earth, do not cover my blood; may my cry for help never be laid to rest.
data.commentary_text:
my blood--that is, my undeserved suffering. He compares himself to one murdered, whose blood the earth refuses to drink up until he is avenged (Gen 4:10-11; Eze 24:1, Eze 24:8; Isa 26:21). The Arabs say that the dew of heaven will not descend on a spot watered with innocent blood (compare Sa2 1:21). 

no place--no resting-place. "May my cry never stop!" May it go abroad! "Earth" in this verse in antithesis to "heaven" (Job 16:19). May my innocence be as well-known to man as it is even now to God!

data.verse_text: Even now my witness is in heaven, and my advocate is on high.
data.commentary_text:
Also now--Even now, when I am so greatly misunderstood on earth, God in heaven is sensible of my innocence. 

record--Hebrew, "in the high places"; Hebrew, "my witness." Amidst all his impatience, Job still trusts in God.

data.verse_text: My friends are my scoffers as my eyes pour out tears to God.
data.commentary_text: Hebrew, "are my scorners"; more forcibly, "my mockers--my friends!" A heart-cutting paradox [UMBREIT]. God alone remains to whom he can look for attestation of his innocence; plaintively with tearful eye, he supplicates for this.

data.verse_text: Oh, that a man might plead with God as he pleads with his neighbor!
data.commentary_text:
one--rather, "He" (God). "Oh, that He would plead for a man (namely, me) against God." Job quaintly says, "God must support me against God; for He makes me to suffer, and He alone knows me to be innocent" [UMBREIT]. So God helped Jacob in wrestling against Himself (compare Job 23:6; Gen 32:25). God in Jesus Christ does plead with God for man (Rom 8:26-27). 

as a man--literally, "the Son of man." A prefiguring of the advocacy of Jesus Christ--a boon longed for by Job (Job 9:33), though the spiritual pregnancy of his own words, designed for all ages, was but little understood by him (Psa 80:17). 

for his neighbour--Hebrew, "friend." Job himself (Job 42:8) pleaded as intercessor for his "friends," though "his scorners" (Job 16:20); so Jesus Christ the Son of man (Luk 23:34); "for friends" (Joh 15:13-15).

data.verse_text: For when only a few years are past I will go the way of no return.
data.commentary_text:
few--literally, "years of number," that is, few, opposed to numberless (Gen 34:30).




Next: Job Chapter 17

data.verse_text: “My spirit is broken; my days are extinguished; the grave awaits me.
data.commentary_text:
JOB'S ANSWER CONTINUED. (Job 17:1-16)

breath . . . corrupt--result of elephantiasis. But UMBREIT, "my strength (spirit) is spent." 

extinct--Life is compared to an expiring light. "The light of my day is extinguished." 

graves--plural, to heighten the force.

data.verse_text: Surely mockers surround me, and my eyes must gaze at their rebellion.
data.commentary_text:
UMBREIT, more emphatically, "had I only not to endure mockery, in the midst of their contentions I (mine eye) would remain quiet." 

eye continue--Hebrew, "tarry all night"; a figure taken from sleep at night, to express undisturbed rest; opposed to (Job 16:20), when the eye of Job is represented as pouring out tears to God without rest.

data.verse_text: Give me, I pray, the pledge You demand. Who else will be my guarantor?
data.commentary_text:
Lay down now--namely, a pledge or security; that is, be my surety; do Thou attest my innocence, since my friends only mock me (Job 17:2). Both litigating parties had to lay down a sum as security before the trial. 

put me in a surety--Provide a surety for me (in the trial) with Thee. A presage of the "surety" (Heb 7:22), or "one Mediator between God and man" (see on Job 16:21). 

strike hands--"who else (save God Himself) could strike hands with me?" that is, be my security (Psa 119:122). The Hebrew strikes the hand of him for whom he goes security (Pro 6:1).

data.verse_text: You have closed their minds to understanding; therefore You will not exalt them.
data.commentary_text:
their heart--The intellect of his friends. 

shalt . . . exalt--Rather imperative, "exalt them not"; allow them not to conquer [UMBREIT], (Isa 6:9-10).

data.verse_text: If a man denounces his friends for a price, the eyes of his children will fail.
data.commentary_text: The Hebrew for "flattery" is "smoothness"; then it came to mean a prey divided by lot, because a smooth stone was used in casting the lots (Deu 18:8), "a portion" (Gen 14:24). Therefore translate, "He that delivers up his friend as a prey (which the conduct of my friends implies that they would do), even the eyes," &c. [NOYES] (Job 11:20). Job says this as to the sinner's children, retorting upon their reproach as to the cutting off of his (Job 5:4; Job 15:30). This accords with the Old Testament dispensation of legal retribution (Exo 20:5).

data.verse_text: He has made me a byword among the people, a man in whose face they spit.
data.commentary_text:
He--God. The poet reverentially suppresses the name of God when speaking of calamities inflicted. 

by-word-- (Deu 28:37; Psa 69:11). My awful punishment makes my name execrated everywhere, as if I must have been superlatively bad to have earned it. 

aforetime . . . tabret--as David was honored (Sa1 18:6). Rather from a different Hebrew root, "I am treated to my face as an object of disgust," literally, "an object to be spit upon in the face" (Num 12:14). So Raca means (Mat 5:22) [UMBREIT].

data.verse_text: My eyes have grown dim with grief, and my whole body is but a shadow.
data.commentary_text:
(Psa 6:7; Psa 31:9; Deu 34:7). 

members--literally, "figures"; all the individual members being peculiar forms of the body; opposed to "shadow," which looks like a figure without solidity.

data.verse_text: The upright are appalled at this, and the innocent are stirred against the godless.
data.commentary_text:
astonied--at my unmerited sufferings. 

against the hypocrite--The upright shall feel their sense of justice wounded ("will be indignant") because of the prosperity of the wicked. By "hypocrite" or "ungodly," he perhaps glances at his false friends.

data.verse_text: Yet a righteous one holds to his way, and the one with clean hands grows stronger.
data.commentary_text: The strength of religious principle is heightened by misfortune. The pious shall take fresh courage to persevere from the example of suffering Job. The image is from a warrior acquiring new courage in action (Isa 40:30-31; Phi 1:14).

data.verse_text: But come back and try again, all of you. For I will not find a wise man among you.
data.commentary_text: return--If you have anything to advance really wise, though I doubt it, recommence your speech. For as yet I cannot find one wise man among you all.

data.verse_text: My days have passed; my plans are broken off— even the desires of my heart.
data.commentary_text:
Only do not vainly speak of the restoration of health to me; for "my days are past." 

broken off--as the threads of the web cut off from the loom (Isa 38:12). 

thoughts--literally, "possessions," that is, all the feelings and fair hopes which my heart once nourished. These belong to the heart, as "purposes" to the understanding; the two together here describe the entire inner man.

data.verse_text: They have turned night into day, making light seem near in the face of darkness.
data.commentary_text:
They--namely, "my friends."
