On the other hand, Job 40:21-22 are characteristic of the amphibious river horse. So leviathan (the twisting animal), Job 41:1, is a generalized term for cetacea, pythons, saurians of the neighboring seas and rivers, including the crocodile, which is the most prominent, and is often associated with the river horse by old writers. "Behemoth" seems to be the Egyptian Pehemout, "water-ox," Hebraized, so-called as being like an ox, whence the Italian bombarino. 

with thee--as I made thyself. Yet how great the difference! The manifold wisdom and power of God! 

he eateth grass--marvellous in an animal living so much in the water; also strange, that such a monster should not be carnivorous.

data.verse_text: See the strength of his loins and the power in the muscles of his belly.
data.commentary_text: navel--rather, "muscles" of his belly; the weakest point of the elephant, therefore it is not meant.

data.verse_text: His tail sways like a cedar; the sinews of his thighs are tightly knit.
data.commentary_text:
like a cedar--As the tempest bends the cedar, so it can move its smooth thick tail [UMBREIT]. But the cedar implies straightness and length, such as do not apply to the river horse's short tail, but perhaps to an extinct species of animal (see on Job 40:15). 

stones--rather, "thighs." 

wrapped--firmly twisted together, like a thick rope.

data.verse_text: His bones are tubes of bronze; his limbs are rods of iron.
data.commentary_text: strong--rather, "tubes" of copper [UMBREIT].

data.verse_text: He is the foremost of God’s works; only his Maker can draw the sword against him.
data.commentary_text:
Chief of the works of God; so "ways" (Job 26:14; Pro 8:22). 

can make his sword to approach--rather, "has furnished him with his sword" (harpe), namely, the sickle-like teeth with which he cuts down grain. English Version, however, is literally right.

data.verse_text: The hills yield him their produce, while all the beasts of the field play nearby.
data.commentary_text:
The mountain is not his usual haunt. BOCHART says it is sometimes found there (?). 

beasts . . . play--a graphic trait: though armed with such teeth, he lets the beasts play near him unhurt, for his food is grass.

data.verse_text: He lies under the lotus plants, hidden among the reeds of the marsh.
data.commentary_text:
lieth--He leads an inactive life. 

shady trees--rather, "lotus bushes"; as Job 40:22 requires.

data.verse_text: The lotus plants conceal him in their shade; the willows of the brook surround him.
data.commentary_text: shady trees--Translate: "lotus bushes."

data.verse_text: Though the river rages, Behemoth is unafraid; he remains secure, though the Jordan surges to his mouth.
data.commentary_text: Rather, "(Though) a river be violent (overflow), he trembleth not"; (for though living on land, he can live in the water, too); he is secure, though a Jordan swell up to his mouth. "Jordan" is used for any great river (consonant with the "behemoth"), being a poetical generalization (see on Job 40:15). The author cannot have been a Hebrew as UMBREIT asserts, or he would not adduce the Jordan, where there were no river horses. He alludes to it as a name for any river, but not as one known to him, except by hearsay.

data.verse_text: Can anyone capture him as he looks on, or pierce his nose with a snare?
data.commentary_text:
Rather, "Will any take him by open force" (literally, "before his eyes"), "or pierce his nose with cords?" No; he can only be taken by guile, and in a pitfall (Job 41:1-2).




Next: Job Chapter 41

data.verse_text: “Can you pull in Leviathan with a hook or tie down his tongue with a rope?
data.commentary_text:
(Job 41:1-34)

leviathan--literally, "the twisted animal," gathering itself in folds: a synonym to the Thannin (Job 3:8, Margin; see Psa 74:14; type of the Egyptian tyrant; Psa 104:26; Isa 27:1; the Babylon tyrant). A poetical generalization for all cetacean, serpentine, and saurian monsters (see on Job 40:15, hence all the description applies to no one animal); especially the crocodile; which is naturally described after the river horse, as both are found in the Nile. 

tongue . . . lettest down?--The crocodile has no tongue, or a very small one cleaving to the lower jaw. But as in fishing the tongue of the fish draws the baited hook to it, God asks, Canst thou in like manner take leviathan?

data.verse_text: Can you put a cord through his nose or pierce his jaw with a hook?
data.commentary_text:
hook--rather, "a rope of rushes." 

thorn--rather, a "ring" or "hook." So wild beasts were led about when caught (Isa 37:29; Eze 29:4); fishes also were secured thus and thrown into the water to keep them alive.

data.verse_text: Will he beg you for mercy or speak to you softly?
data.commentary_text: soft words--that thou mayest spare his life. No: he is untamable.

data.verse_text: Will he make a covenant with you to take him as a slave for life?
data.commentary_text: Can he be tamed for domestic use (so Job 39:10-12)?

data.verse_text: Can you pet him like a bird or put him on a leash for your maidens?
data.commentary_text: a bird?--that is, tamed.

data.verse_text: Will traders barter for him or divide him among the merchants?
data.commentary_text:
Rather, "partners" (namely, in fishing). 

make a banquet--The parallelism rather supports UMBREIT, "Do partners (in trade) desire to purchase him?" So the Hebrew (Deu 2:6). 

merchants--literally, "Canaanites," who were great merchants (Hos 12:7, Margin).

data.verse_text: Can you fill his hide with harpoons or his head with fishing spears?
data.commentary_text: His hide is not penetrable, as that of fishes.

data.verse_text: If you lay a hand on him, you will remember the battle and never repeat it!
data.commentary_text: If thou lay . . . thou wilt have reason ever to remember . . . and thou wilt never try it again.

data.verse_text: Surely hope of overcoming him is false. Is not the sight of him overwhelming?
data.commentary_text:
the hope--of taking him. 

cast down--with fear "at the (mere) sight of him."

data.verse_text: No one is so fierce as to rouse Leviathan. Then who is able to stand against Me?
data.commentary_text: fierce--courageous. If a man dare attack one of My creatures (Gen 49:9; Num 24:9), who will dare (as Job has wished) oppose himself (Psa 2:2) to Me, the Creator? This is the main drift of the description of leviathan.

data.verse_text: Who has given to Me that I should repay him? Everything under heaven is Mine.
data.commentary_text: prevented--done Me a favor first: anticipated Me with service (Psa 21:3). None can call Me to account ("stand before Me," Job 41:10) as unjust, because I have withdrawn favors from him (as in Job's case): for none has laid Me under a prior obligation by conferring on Me something which was not already My own. What can man give to Him who possesses all, including man himself? Man cannot constrain the creature to be his "servant" (Job 41:4), much less the Creator.

data.verse_text: I cannot keep silent about his limbs, his power and graceful form.
data.commentary_text:
I will not conceal--a resumption of the description broken off by the digression, which formed an agreeable change. 

his power--literally, "the way," that is, true proportion or expression of his strength (so Hebrew, Deu 19:4). 

comely proportion--literally, "the comeliness of his structure" (his apparatus: so "suit of apparel" Jdg 17:10) [MAURER]. UMBREIT translates, "his armor." But that follows after.

data.verse_text: Who can strip off his outer coat? Who can approach him with a bridle?
data.commentary_text:
discover--rather, "uncover the surface" of his garment (skin, Job 10:11): strip off the hard outer coat with which the inner skin is covered. 

with--rather, "within his double jaws"; literally, "bridle"; hence that into which the bridle is put, the double row of teeth; but "bridle" is used to imply that none dare put his hand in to insert a bridle where in other animals it is placed (Job 41:4; Job 39:10).

data.verse_text: Who can open his jaws, ringed by his fearsome teeth?
data.commentary_text: doors of . . . face--his mouth. His teeth are sixty in number, larger in proportion than his body, some standing out, some serrated, fitting into each other like a comb [BOCHART].

data.verse_text: His rows of scales are his pride, tightly sealed together. One scale is so near to another that no air can pass between them. They are joined to one another; they clasp and cannot be separated.
data.commentary_text:
Rather, his "furrows of shields" (as "tubes," "channels," see on Job 40:18), are, &c., that is, the rows of scales, like shields covering him: he has seventeen such rows. 

shut up--firmly closed together. A musket ball cannot penetrate him, save in the eye, throat, and belly.

data.verse_text: His snorting flashes with light, and his eyes are like the rays of dawn.
data.commentary_text:
Translate: "his sneezing, causeth a light to shine." Amphibious animals, emerging after having long held their breath under water, respire by violently expelling the breath like one sneezing: in the effort the eyes which are usually directed towards the sun, seem to flash fire; or it is the expelled breath that, in the sun, seems to emit light. 

eyelids of morning--The Egyptian hieroglyphics paint the eyes of the crocodile as the symbol for morning, because the eyes appear the first thing, before the whole body emerges from the deep [HorÃ&brvbr HierogliphicÃ&brvbr 1.65. BOCHART].

data.verse_text: Firebrands stream from his mouth; fiery sparks shoot forth!
data.commentary_text: burning lamps--"torches"; namely, in respiring (Job 41:18), seem to go out.

data.verse_text: Smoke billows from his nostrils as from a boiling pot over burning reeds.
data.commentary_text: seething--boiling: literally, "blown under," under which a fire is blown.

data.verse_text: His breath sets coals ablaze, and flames pour from his mouth.
data.commentary_text: kindleth coals--poetical imagery (Psa 18:8).

data.verse_text: Strength resides in his neck, and dismay leaps before him.
data.commentary_text:
remaineth--abideth permanently. His chief strength is in the neck. 

sorrow--anxiety or dismay personified. 

is turned into joy--rather, "danceth," "exulteth"; wherever he goes, he spreads terror "before him."

data.verse_text: The folds of his flesh are tightly joined; they are firm and immovable.
data.commentary_text:
flakes--rather, "dewlaps"; that which falls down (Margin). They are "joined" fast and firm, together, not hanging loose, as in the ox. 

are firm--UMBREIT and MAURER, "are spread." 

in themselves--rather, "upon him."

data.verse_text: His chest is as hard as a rock, as hard as a lower millstone!
data.commentary_text: heart--"In large beasts which are less acute in feeling, there is great firmness of the heart, and slower motion" [BOCHART]. The nether millstone, on which the upper turns, is especially hard.

data.verse_text: When Leviathan rises up, the mighty are terrified; they withdraw before his thrashing.
data.commentary_text:
he--the crocodile; a type of the awe which the Creator inspires when He rises in wrath. 

breakings--namely, of the mind, that is, terror. 

purify themselves--rather, "they wander from the way," that is, flee away bewildered [MAURER and UMBREIT].

data.verse_text: The sword that reaches him has no effect, nor does the spear or dart or arrow.
data.commentary_text:
cannot hold--on his hard skin. 

habergeon--coat of mail; avail must be taken by zeugma out of "hold," as the verb in the second clause: "hold" cannot apply to the "coat of mail."

data.verse_text: He regards iron as straw and bronze as rotten wood.
data.commentary_text: iron . . . brass--namely, weapons.

data.verse_text: No arrow can make him flee; slingstones become like chaff to him.
data.commentary_text:
arrow--literally, "son of the bow"; Oriental imagery (Lam 3:13; Margin). 

stubble--Arrows produce no more effect than it would to throw stubble at him.

data.verse_text: A club is regarded as straw, and he laughs at the sound of the lance.
data.commentary_text: Darts--rather, "clubs"; darts have been already mentioned (Job 41:26).

data.verse_text: His undersides are jagged potsherds, spreading out the mud like a threshing sledge.
data.commentary_text:
stones--rather, "potsherds," that is, the sharp and pointed scales on the belly, like broken pieces of pottery. 

sharp-pointed things--rather, "a threshing instrument," but not on the fruits of the earth, but "on the mire"; irony. When he lies on the mire, he leaves the marks of his scales so imprinted on it, that one might fancy a threshing instrument with its sharp teeth had been drawn over it (Isa 28:27).

data.verse_text: He makes the depths seethe like a cauldron; he makes the sea like a jar of ointment.
data.commentary_text:
Whenever he moves. 

sea--the Nile (Isa 19:5; Nah 3:8). 

pot of ointment--the vessel in which it is mixed. Appropriate to the crocodile, which emits a musky smell.

data.verse_text: He leaves a glistening wake behind him; one would think the deep had white hair!
data.commentary_text:
path--the foam on his track. 

hoary--as hair of the aged.

data.verse_text: Nothing on earth is his equal— a creature devoid of fear!
data.commentary_text: who--being one who, &c.

data.verse_text: He looks down on all the haughty; he is king over all the proud.”
data.commentary_text:
beholdeth--as their superior. 

children of pride--the proud and fierce beasts. So Job 28:8; Hebrew, "sons of pride." To humble the pride of man and to teach implicit submission, is the aim of Jehovah's speech and of the book; therefore with this as to leviathan, the type of God in His lordship over creation, He closes.




Next: Job Chapter 42

data.verse_text: Then Job replied to the LORD: “I know that You can do all things and that no plan of Yours can be thwarted.
data.commentary_text:
JOB'S PENITENT REPLY. (Job 42:1-6)

In the first clause he owns God to be omnipotent over nature, as contrasted with his own feebleness, which God had proved (Job 40:15; Job 41:34); in the second, that God is supremely just (which, in order to be governor of the world, He must needs be) in all His dealings, as contrasted with his own vileness (Job 42:6), and incompetence to deal with the wicked as a just judge (Job 40:8-14). 

thought--"purpose," as in Job 17:11; but it is usually applied to evil devices (Job 21:27; Psa 10:2): the ambiguous word is designedly chosen to express that, while to Job's finite view, God's plans seem bad, to the All-wise One they continue unhindered in their development, and will at last be seen to be as good as they are infinitely wise. No evil can emanate from the Parent of good (Jam 1:13, Jam 1:17); but it is His prerogative to overrule evil to good.

data.verse_text: You asked, ‘Who is this who conceals My counsel without knowledge?’ Surely I spoke of things I did not understand, things too wonderful for me to know.
data.commentary_text:
I am the man! Job in God's own words (Job 38:2) expresses his deep and humble penitence. God's word concerning our guilt should be engraven on our hearts and form the groundwork of our confession. Most men in confessing sin palliate rather than confess. Job in omitting "by words" (Job 38:2), goes even further than God's accusation. Not merely my words, but my whole thoughts and ways were "without knowledge." 

too wonderful--I rashly denied that Thou hast any fixed plan in governing human affairs, merely because Thy plan was "too wonderful" for my comprehension.

data.verse_text: You said, ‘Listen now, and I will speak. I will question you, and you shall inform Me.’
data.commentary_text: When I said, "Hear," &c., Job's demand (Job 13:22) convicted him of being "without knowledge." God alone could speak thus to Job, not Job to God: therefore he quotes again God's words as the groundwork of retracting his own foolish words.

data.verse_text: My ears had heard of You, but now my eyes have seen You.
data.commentary_text:
hearing of the ear-- (Psa 18:44, Margin). Hearing and seeing are often in antithesis (Job 29:11; Psa 18:8). 

seeth--not God's face (Exo 33:20), but His presence in the veil of a dark cloud (Job 38:1). Job implies also that, besides this literal seeing, he now saw spiritually what he had indistinctly taken on hearsay before God's infinite wisdom. He "now" proves this; he had seen in a literal sense before, at the beginning of God's speech, but he had not seen spiritually till "now" at its close.

data.verse_text: Therefore I retract my words, and I repent in dust and ashes.”
data.commentary_text: myself--rather "I abhor," and retract the rash speeches I made against thee (Job 42:3-4) [UMBREIT].

data.verse_text: After the LORD had spoken these words to Job, He said to Eliphaz the Temanite, “My wrath is kindled against you and your two friends. For you have not spoken about Me accurately, as My servant Job has.
data.commentary_text:
EPILOGUE, in prose. (Job 42:7-17)

to Eliphaz--because he was the foremost of the three friends; their speeches were but the echo of his. 

right--literally, "well-grounded," sure and true. Their spirit towards Job was unkindly, and to justify themselves in their unkindliness they used false arguments (Job 13:7); (namely, that calamities always prove peculiar guilt); therefore, though it was "for God" they spake thus falsely, God "reproves" them, as Job said He would (Job 13:10). 

as . . . Job hath--Job had spoken rightly in relation to them and their argument, denying their theory, and the fact which they alleged, that he was peculiarly guilty and a hypocrite; but wrongly in relation to God, when he fell into the opposite extreme of almost denying all guilt. This extreme he has now repented of, and therefore God speaks of him as now altogether "right."

data.verse_text: So now, take seven bulls and seven rams, go to My servant Job, and sacrifice a burnt offering for yourselves. Then My servant Job will pray for you, for I will accept his prayer and not deal with you according to your folly. For you have not spoken accurately about Me, as My servant Job has.”
data.commentary_text:
seven--(See Introduction). The number offered by the Gentile prophet (Num 23:1). Job plainly lived before the legal priesthood, &c. The patriarchs acted as priests for their families; and sometimes as praying mediators (Gen 20:17), thus foreshadowing the true Mediator (Ti1 2:5), but sacrifice accompanies and is the groundwork on which the mediation rests. 

him--rather, "His person [face] only" (see on Job 22:30). The "person," must be first accepted, before God can accept his offering and work (Gen 4:4); that can be only through Jesus Christ. 

folly--impiety (Job 1:22; Job 2:10).

data.verse_text: So Eliphaz the Temanite, Bildad the Shuhite, and Zophar the Naamathite went and did as the LORD had told them; and the LORD accepted Job’s request.
data.commentary_text: The forgiving spirit of Job foreshadows the love of Jesus Christ and of Christians to enemies (Mat 5:44; Luk 23:34; Act 7:60; Act 16:24, Act 16:28, Act 16:30-31).

data.verse_text: After Job had prayed for his friends, the LORD restored his prosperity and doubled his former possessions.
data.commentary_text:
turned . . . captivity--proverbial for restored, or amply indemnified him for all he had lost (Eze 16:53; Psa 14:7; Hos 6:11). Thus the future vindication of man, body and soul, against Satan (Job 1:9-12), at the resurrection (Job 19:25-27), has its earnest and adumbration in the temporal vindication of Job at last by Jehovah in person. 

twice--so to the afflicted literal and spiritual Jerusalem (Isa 40:2; Isa 60:7; Isa 61:7; Zac 9:12). As in Job's case, so in that of Jesus Christ, the glorious recompense follows the "intercession" for enemies (Isa 53:12).

data.verse_text: All his brothers and sisters and prior acquaintances came and dined with him in his house. They consoled him and comforted him over all the adversity that the LORD had brought upon him. And each one gave him a piece of silver and a gold ring.
data.commentary_text:
It was Job's complaint in his misery that his "brethren," were "estranged" from him (Job 19:13); these now return with the return of his prosperity (Pro 14:20; Pro 19:6-7); the true friend loveth at all times (Pro 17:17; Pro 18:24). "Swallow friends leave in the winter and return with the spring" [HENRY]. 

eat bread--in token of friendship (Psa 41:9). 

piece of money--Presents are usual in visiting a man of rank in the East, especially after a calamity (Ch2 32:23). Hebrew, kesita. MAGEE translates "a lamb" (the medium of exchange then before money was used), as it is in Margin of Gen 33:19; Jos 24:32. But it is from the Arabic kasat, "weighed out" [UMBREIT], not coined; so Gen 42:35; Gen 33:19; compare with Gen 23:15, makes it likely it was equal to four shekels; Hebrew kashat, "pure," namely, metal. The term, instead of the usual "shekel," &c., is a mark of antiquity. 

earring--whether for the nose or ear (Gen 35:4; Isa 3:21). Much of the gold in the East, in the absence of banks, is in the shape of ornaments.

data.verse_text: So the LORD blessed Job’s latter days more than his first. He owned 14,000 sheep, 6,000 camels, 1,000 yoke of oxen, and 1,000 female donkeys.
data.commentary_text: Probably by degrees, not all at once.

data.verse_text: And he also had seven sons and three daughters.
data.commentary_text: The same number as before, Job 1:2; perhaps by a second wife; in Job 19:17 his wife is last mentioned.

data.verse_text: He named his first daughter Jemimah, his second Keziah, and his third Keren-happuch.
data.commentary_text:
Names significant of his restored prosperity (Gen 4:25; Gen 5:29). 

Jemima--"daylight," after his "night" of calamity; but MAURER, "a dove." 

Kezia--"cassia," an aromatic herb (Psa 45:8), instead of his offensive breath and ulcers. 

Keren-happuch--"horn of stibium," a paint with which females dyed their eyelids; in contrast to his "horn defiled in the dust" (Job 16:15). The names also imply the beauty of his daughters.

data.verse_text: No women as beautiful as Job’s daughters could be found in all the land, and their father granted them an inheritance among their brothers.
data.commentary_text: inheritance among . . . brethren--An unusual favor in the East to daughters, who, in the Jewish law, only inherited, if there were no sons (Num 27:8), a proof of wealth and unanimity.

data.verse_text: After this, Job lived 140 years and saw his children and their children to the fourth generation.
data.commentary_text:
The Septuagint makes Job live a hundred seventy years after his calamity, and two hundred forty in all. This would make him seventy at the time of his calamity, which added to a hundred forty in Hebrew text makes up two hundred ten; a little more than the age (two hundred five) of Terah, father of Abraham, perhaps his contemporary. Man's length of life gradually shortened, till it reached threescore and ten in Moses' time (Psa 90:10). 

sons' sons--a proof of divine favor (Gen 50:23; Psa 128:6; Pro 17:6).

data.verse_text: And so Job died, old and full of years.
data.commentary_text:
full of days--fully sated and contented with all the happiness that life could give him; realizing what Eliphaz had painted as the lot of the godly (Job 5:26; Psa 91:16; Gen 25:8; Gen 35:29). The Septuagint adds, "It is written, that he will rise again with those whom the Lord will raise up." Compare Mat 27:52-53, from which it perhaps was derived spuriously.





Next: Psalms Introduction
